<skills>
Skills provide specialized capabilities, domain knowledge, and refined workflows for producing high-quality outputs. Each skill folder contains tested instructions for specific domains like testing strategies, API design, or performance optimization. Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.
BLOCKING REQUIREMENT: When a skill applies to the user's request, you MUST load and read the SKILL.md file IMMEDIATELY as your first action, BEFORE generating any other response or taking action on the task. Use #tool:read/readFile to load the relevant skill(s).
NEVER just mention or reference a skill in your response without actually reading it first. If a skill is relevant, load it before proceeding.
How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request
2. If any skill's domain overlaps with the task, load that skill immediately
3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills
Examples:
- "Help me write unit tests for this module" -> Load the testing skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Optimize this slow function" -> Load the performance-profiling skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Add a discount code field to checkout" -> Load both the checkout-flow and form-validation skills FIRST

response.value:
The ACR is created as part of the Bicep deployment in **Exercise 01, Task 03**. You need to run `azd up` from the repo root first — it provisions all the Azure resources (including the Container Registry, Cosmos DB, AI Services, Container Apps, etc.) via the Bicep template.

From the repo root:

```bash
# Login (use --use-device-code in Codespaces)
az login --use-device-code
azd auth login --use-device-code

# Optionally check quota first
python src/infra/check_quota.py

# Deploy all infrastructure
azd up
```

`azd up` will prompt you for an environment name, subscription, and region. It creates a resource group `rg-<environment-name>` and deploys all resources defined in
, which includes the ACR. Once that completes, you can run your `az acr build` command using the actual ACR name from the deployment output.

thinking.text: The user is trying to build a Docker image using Azure Container Registry (ACR), but the ACR resource `acraiagents01` doesn't exist yet. They're working through a workshop. Let me check the workshop docs to understand what setup steps they should have completed first.

toolCallRounds.response:
The ACR is created as part of the Bicep deployment in **Exercise 01, Task 03**. You need to run `azd up` from the repo root first — it provisions all the Azure resources (including the Container Registry, Cosmos DB, AI Services, Container Apps, etc.) via the Bicep template.

From the repo root:

```bash
# Login (use --use-device-code in Codespaces)
az login --use-device-code
azd auth login --use-device-code

# Optionally check quota first
python src/infra/check_quota.py

# Deploy all infrastructure
azd up
```

`azd up` will prompt you for an environment name, subscription, and region. It creates a resource group `rg-<environment-name>` and deploys all resources defined in [src/infra/DeployAzureResources.bicep](src/infra/DeployAzureResources.bicep), which includes the ACR. Once that completes, you can run your `az acr build` command using the actual ACR name from the deployment output.

children.text: Before you can start building your AI shopping assistant, you need to deploy the necessary Azure resources. This includes setting up a resource group, deploying a Bicep script to create the required infrastructure, and configuring environment variables for your application.

In this task, you will deploy a Bicep script that sets up the necessary Azure resources for your AI shopping assistant. This includes creating a resource group and deploying the required infrastructure. In addition, you will install any software prerequisites needed for the application to run.
- You have installed software necessary for the application to run.
- You have created a resource group in Azure.
- You have deployed a Bicep script to create the necessary Azure resources.

- Cloning a repository via the [command line](https://docs.github.com/en/github/creating-cloning-and-archiving-repositories/cloning-a-repository) or [GitHub Desktop](https://docs.github.com/en/desktop/contributing-and-collaborating-using-github-desktop/cloning-a-repository-from-github-to-github-desktop)
- [Create Bicep files by using Visual Studio Code](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/azure-resource-manager/bicep/visual-studio-code?tabs=CLI)

Fork [this GitHub repository](https://github.com/microsoft/TechWorkshop-L300-AI-Apps-and-Agents) to your account. You will need to fork the repo in order to modify and use GitHub Actions workflows in later tasks.
Expand this section to view the solution
In order to fork this repository, make sure that you are signed in to GitHub with the account you would like to use. Then, select the **Fork** button.
On the next page, select your account as the Owner and leave the repository name alone. Then, select **Create fork** to complete the process.
![Complete the process of forking the GitHub repository](../../media/Solution/0101_Fork2.png)
### 02: Run Codespace or install necessary software
To work through this training, you can either use a GitHub Codespace or install the necessary software on your local machine.
To use a GitHub Codespace, navigate to your forked repository and select the **Code** button. Then, select the **Codespaces** tab and choose **Create codespace on main**.
This Codespace is pre-configured with all the necessary software to complete this training.
Alternatively, you can install the necessary software on your local machine. This includes:
- [A Git client](https://git-scm.com/download/). An alternative option is to install [GitHub Desktop](https://desktop.github.com/).

> As of the latest update (April 2026), not all of the packages in this demo support Python 3.14. Python 3.12 and 3.13 are good choices for Python versions, as all of the packages you will use support these versions.
If you do not use a Codespace, you will also need to clone the repository to your local machine.
You can do this using the following command:
Make sure to replace `<your-username>` with your actual GitHub username.

After you have set up your development environment, the next step is to deploy the necessary Azure resources. You will use the [Azure Developer CLI (azd)](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/developer/azure-developer-cli/overview) to provision infrastructure. The `azd` tool will create a resource group, deploy the Bicep template, and automatically filter available regions based on resource availability and OpenAI model quota.
Expand this section to view the solution
First, ensure that you are logged into both the Azure CLI and the Azure Developer CLI. You can do so using the following commands on a local machine:

This training will have you work through a customer proof of concept regarding Microsoft Foundry. The scenario entails building a proof of concept for Zava, a retail chain that specializes in "do-it-yourself" solutions for home improvement projects. Zava would like to build a shopping assistant that helps customers choose the correct products for their own home improvement projects. Customers will be able to upload images, ask questions regarding the set of products available from Zava, and make purchases, all from a multimodal chat interface.

In this first exercise, we will set the stage for Zava by deploying out resources needed for the proof of concept, as well as ensuring that we have the client tools necessary to run through the remaining exercises.
After you complete this exercise, you will be able to:
* Use a Bicep script to provision Azure resources
* Load data into Cosmos DB and Azure AI Search
* Create resources in Microsoft Foundry and connect Azure resources to an existing project

> If you receive an error message reading, "Your sign-in was successful but does not meet the criteria to access this resource," you are likely using a Microsoft EMU account or some other account that has stringent restrictions on machine access. You will need to use an external MCAPS subscription or some other paid subscription to continue. A Visual Studio subscription will **not** be sufficient, as these accounts will likely not have access to Microsoft Foundry and the language models we will use.

Before deploying, you can optionally run the pre-flight quota check script to verify that your subscription has the required resource providers registered and available in the candidate regions (Cosmos DB, Container Registry, AI Services, Container Apps):
# Check the default regions (eastus2, swedencentral, francecentral)
# Or add additional regions to check
Once you have confirmed a viable region, from the root directory of the repository, run:
The `azd up` command will prompt you for:
1. **Environment name** -- enter a name such as `techworkshop-l300-ai-agents`.
2. **Azure subscription** -- select the subscription you would like to use.

3. **Azure location** -- select a region. The location picker will only show regions where required resources are available and sufficient OpenAI model quota exists. Recommended regions include **East US 2**, **Sweden Central**, and **France Central**.

> If none of the recommended regions appear in the list, your account may not have sufficient quota. You can check your quota in the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com/) under **Subscriptions > Usage + quotas**. If you need to use a region outside the recommended list, be aware that you may not be able to run the Red Teaming agent in Exercise 06.

The `azd up` command will create a resource group named `rg-<environment-name>` and deploy all required infrastructure. You can monitor the progress of the deployment in the terminal output, which includes a link to the deployment in the Azure portal.
![Monitor the progress of the deployment in the Azure portal.](../../media/Solution/0101_Bicep6.png)

The final key task is to enable Vector Search for NoSQL API in your Cosmos DB account. This may take 15 or more minutes to become active, so enabling this now will ensure that the setting is enabled when you need it in Exercise 1 Task 3.

> The Bicep script you have run already includes a command to enable Vector Search for the Comsos DB NoSQL API, but this command does not always work as expected (or in a timely fashion). If you see that vector search is already enabled, you do not need to re-enable it--you are already good to continue.
Expand this section to view the solution
Navigate to the [Azure portal](https://portal.azure.com/) and open the Cosmos DB account that you created.

Before you can create the container that you will use for this training, you will need to enable vector search for the Cosmos DB NoSQL API. To do so, navigate to the **Settings** section of your Cosmos DB account and select **Features**. Then, select the **Vector Search for NoSQL API** option.
![Update the Vector Search for NoSQL API setting.](../../media/Solution/0103_CosmosDB1.png)
Select the **Enable** button to enable vector search. This may take up to 15 minutes for the policy to become active.

<skills>
Skills provide specialized capabilities, domain knowledge, and refined workflows for producing high-quality outputs. Each skill folder contains tested instructions for specific domains like testing strategies, API design, or performance optimization. Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.
BLOCKING REQUIREMENT: When a skill applies to the user's request, you MUST load and read the SKILL.md file IMMEDIATELY as your first action, BEFORE generating any other response or taking action on the task. Use #tool:read/readFile to load the relevant skill(s).
NEVER just mention or reference a skill in your response without actually reading it first. If a skill is relevant, load it before proceeding.
How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request
2. If any skill's domain overlaps with the task, load that skill immediately
3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills
Examples:
- "Help me write unit tests for this module" -> Load the testing skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Optimize this slow function" -> Load the performance-profiling skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Add a discount code field to checkout" -> Load both the checkout-flow and form-validation skills FIRST

<skills>
Skills provide specialized capabilities, domain knowledge, and refined workflows for producing high-quality outputs. Each skill folder contains tested instructions for specific domains like testing strategies, API design, or performance optimization. Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.
BLOCKING REQUIREMENT: When a skill applies to the user's request, you MUST load and read the SKILL.md file IMMEDIATELY as your first action, BEFORE generating any other response or taking action on the task. Use #tool:read/readFile to load the relevant skill(s).
NEVER just mention or reference a skill in your response without actually reading it first. If a skill is relevant, load it before proceeding.
How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request
2. If any skill's domain overlaps with the task, load that skill immediately
3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills
Examples:
- "Help me write unit tests for this module" -> Load the testing skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Optimize this slow function" -> Load the performance-profiling skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Add a discount code field to checkout" -> Load both the checkout-flow and form-validation skills FIRST

response.value:
Got it. Now I have all the values. The AI Services base endpoint is `https://aif-pprta2o2djmjy.cognitiveservices.azure.com/` and the Foundry project endpoint is `https://aif-pprta2o2djmjy.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/proj-pprta2o2djmjy`. All four env vars (`gpt_endpoint`, `embedding_endpoint`, `phi_4_endpoint`) point to the same AI Services endpoint since all models are deployed under the same account.

Here's your command:

```bash
az containerapp update \
  --name pprta2o2djmjy-app \
  --resource-group rg-techworkshop-l300-ai-agents \
```

toolCallRounds.response: The Container App's managed identity needs a role assignment on the AI Services account. Let me check and fix this.

thinking.text:
The user is getting a permission denied error when trying to use the chatbot deployed in a Docker container (Container App). The error says the principal `834a6258-611d-4f35-bcc7-e77898fd9a96` lacks the `Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/AIServices/agents/write` data action. This is the managed identity of the Container App that needs to be granted the appropriate role on the AI Services/Foundry resource.

Let me check what role assignments are needed and find the Container App's managed identity.

toolCalls.name: run_in_terminal
toolCalls.arguments: {"command": "az role assignment list --assignee 834a6258-611d-4f35-bcc7-e77898fd9a96 --scope \"/subscriptions/3ed771bb-e197-497f-807b-6a01991cc705/resourceGroups/rg-techworkshop-l300-ai-agents/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/aif-pprta2o2djmjy\" --query \"[].{role:roleDefinitionName}\" -o table 2>/dev/null", "explanation": "Check what roles the Container App identity currently has on the AI Services account.", "goal": "Check existing role assignments", "mode": "sync", "timeout": 15000}

toolCallRounds.response: The identity only has **Cognitive Services OpenAI User**, which doesn't cover the Foundry agents API. It needs the **Azure AI Developer** role. Let me assign it:

toolCalls.name: run_in_terminal
toolCalls.arguments: {"command": "az role assignment create \\\n  --assignee 834a6258-611d-4f35-bcc7-e77898fd9a96 \\\n  --role \"Azure AI Developer\" \\\n  --scope \"/subscriptions/3ed771bb-e197-497f-807b-6a01991cc705/resourceGroups/rg-techworkshop-l300-ai-agents/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/aif-pprta2o2djmjy\" \\\n  -o table 2>/dev/null", "explanation": "Assign the Azure AI Developer role to the Container App's managed identity on the AI Services account.", "goal": "Grant Foundry agents permission", "mode": "sync", "timeout": 15000}

toolCallRounds.response:

Role assignments can take a minute or two to propagate. After that, try your question again and it should work.

Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.
BLOCKING REQUIREMENT: When a skill applies to the user's request, you MUST load and read the SKILL.md file IMMEDIATELY as your first action, BEFORE generating any other response or taking action on the task. Use #tool:read/readFile to load the relevant skill(s).

How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request

3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills

<skills>
Skills provide specialized capabilities, domain knowledge, and refined workflows for producing high-quality outputs. Each skill folder contains tested instructions for specific domains like testing strategies, API design, or performance optimization. Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.
BLOCKING REQUIREMENT: When a skill applies to the user's request, you MUST load and read the SKILL.md file IMMEDIATELY as your first action, BEFORE generating any other response or taking action on the task. Use #tool:read/readFile to load the relevant skill(s).
NEVER just mention or reference a skill in your response without actually reading it first. If a skill is relevant, load it before proceeding.
How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request
2. If any skill's domain overlaps with the task, load that skill immediately
3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills
Examples:
- "Help me write unit tests for this module" -> Load the testing skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Optimize this slow function" -> Load the performance-profiling skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Add a discount code field to checkout" -> Load both the checkout-flow and form-validation skills FIRST

Now I see the issue. The endpoint URLs are different for each variable:

1. `FOUNDRY_ENDPOINT`: The project endpoint from Foundry
2. `gpt_endpoint`: `https://aif-pprta2o2djmjy.cognitiveservices.azure.com` (everything prior to `/openai`)
3. `embedding_endpoint`: The full Target URI from the text-embedding-3-large deployment
4. `phi_4_endpoint`: `https://aif-pprta2o2djmjy.services.ai.azure.com/models`

But the main issue is the permission denied error. 

I'm wondering if the Container App is missing the `Azure AI User` role that the Bicep template assigns to user principals and Cosmos DB. Looking at the second error more carefully — "Principal does not have access to API/Operation" — it's different from the initial agents write permission error. The Azure AI Developer role should handle that specific permission, but there might be another access issue I'm not catching.

Comparing the role assignments across identities in the Bicep, the Container App only has `Cognitive Services OpenAI User` on both project and Foundry account, while Cosmos DB and user principals get additional roles like `Azure AI User` and `Cognitive Services Contributor`. I should add `Azure AI User` to the Container App since that's likely what the Foundry agents API requires.

Wait, I already added `Azure AI Developer` which should be a superset of `Azure AI User`, but let me also try adding `Cognitive Services Contributor` in case that's needed for the conversations API. I should verify the role ID for `Azure AI Developer` is correct and check what data actions it includes.

Actually, the "Principal does not have access to API/Operation" error might not be about roles at all — it could be that the `FOUNDRY_ENDPOINT` value is incorrect. The code calls `project_client.get_openai_client()` and then uses Foundry agent APIs, so I need to verify what endpoint format the `AIProjectClient` actually expects versus what I'm passing.

Looking at the REST API response, the project endpoint was `https://aif-pprta2o2djmjy.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/proj-pprta2o2djmjy`, which seems like the right format for `AIProjectClient`. But I'm wondering if the issue is actually that the agents like "cora" haven't been created in Foundry yet — though that would probably throw a different error. Given that the user is already testing the deployed Container App, this really does seem like a permissions issue rather than missing resources.

I think I need to try adding the `Azure AI User` role in addition to `Azure AI Developer`. The Bicep template already uses this role for other identities, so it might cover a different part of the data plane that's needed for the agents to work properly.

Wait a minute or two for the new revision to become active, then try your question again.

Agent service: factory + cache for AgentProcessor instances.
Why a factory? Creating an AgentProcessor involves looking up the agent in
Microsoft Foundry and setting up conversation state. This module caches
processors by (agent_type, agent_id) so that repeated requests for the same
agent reuse the existing processor rather than reinitializing each time.
The thread_id is updated on each call because a new WebSocket session may
want to continue with the same agent but on a fresh conversation thread.
"""Return a cached AgentProcessor, or create and cache a new one.
agent_id: The agent name/ID as registered in Microsoft Foundry.
agent_type: Logical type used for tool assignment (e.g. "cora", "inventory_agent").
thread_id: Conversation thread to continue, or None for a new thread.
project_client: An AIProjectClient connected to your Foundry project.
An AgentProcessor ready to run conversations.
AgentProcessor: manages conversation lifecycle with Microsoft Foundry agents.
- Creating and managing conversation threads via the OpenAI Responses API
- Dispatching MCP tool calls when the agent requests function execution
- Extracting text responses from the agent's output
MCP tool wrappers live in mcp_tools.py; FunctionTool definitions live in
tool_definitions.py. This file focuses solely on conversation orchestration.
# Thread pool for running sync OpenAI SDK calls from async context
"""Orchestrates a conversation with a Microsoft Foundry agent.
Each AgentProcessor is bound to a specific agent (by ID and type) and
manages a conversation thread. When the agent requests a function call,
the processor dispatches it to the corresponding MCP tool wrapper and
feeds the result back to the agent.
def __init__(self, project_client, assistant_id: str, agent_type: str, thread_id=None):
"""Synchronous generator that yields streamed text chunks."""
"""Dispatch function calls from agent output to MCP tool handlers."""

...hould be triggered on a push to the main branch. The Container App already has its environment variables configured in Azure, so the workflow does not need to handle any .env file or secrets injection. After pushing the image, the workflow should update the Container App to use the new image. Use the GitHub commit SHA as the container image tag instead of something like "latest". In addition, the Container App deployment must set four environment variables based off of GitHub secrets. They are: FOUNDRY_ENDPOINT, gpt_endpoint, embedding_endpoint, and phi_4_endpoint.
- `FOUNDRY_ENDPOINT`: The URL of your Microsoft Foundry endpoint as exists in your `.env` file.

Calls az rest --method POST to deploy the agent version to the Foundry REST API at ${FOUNDRY_ENDPOINT}/agents/{agent-name}/versions?api-version=2025-11-15-preview with --resource "https://ai.azure.com"
- `FOUNDRY_ENDPOINT`: The Microsoft Foundry project endpoint (the same value you configured in Exercise 01).

In order to run the code in this training, you will need to set up environment variables for your application. For **local development**, you will create a `.env` file. For **production deployment** to Azure Container Apps, most variables are already configured by the Bicep template, so you only need to set the model-specific endpoints after deploying models in the previous step.

> This training uses **managed identities** (via `DefaultAzureCredential`) to authenticate to all Azure services. You do not need to configure any API keys or connection strings. The only values you need to provide are endpoints, deployment names, and non-secret configuration.
Expand this section to view the solution

Copy the file `src/env_sample.txt` to a new file named `.env` in the same directory. Then, open the `.env` file in a text editor and update the values for each environment variable to match your Azure resources. For any environment variables with pre-filled values, you can leave them as they are unless you explicitly changed the names of the resources during deployment.
You will find the model endpoint variables in the Microsoft Foundry project that you created. To find these values, start by navigating to the **Home** page.
![Review the endpoints and keys associated with your Microsoft Foundry project.](../../media/Solution/0102_FoundryEndpoints1.png)
For the following variables, navigate to the **Models** menu option from the **Build** menu.
![Select each model deployment to collect connection details.](../../media/Solution/0102_FoundryEndpoints2.png)

Then, select the appropriate model deployment that you created in the previous step and navigate to the **Details** tab in order to fill in the details below. Start with the **gpt-5.4-mini** deployment.

- `gpt_endpoint`: Copy the **Target URI** box from the **gpt-5.4-mini** deployment and save everything prior to `/openai` in the URI. It should look something like `https://{YOUR_AI_FOUNDRY}.cognitiveservices.azure.com`. If the Target URI looks like your Foundry endpoint, please use `https://{YOUR_AI_FOUNDRY}.cognitiveservices.azure.com` instead.
Repeat this process to collect the text-embedding-3-large endpoint details:
- `embedding_endpoint`: Copy the **Target URI** box from the **text-embedding-3-large** deployment. You can copy and paste the entire URL for this endpoint.
Repeat this process to collect the Phi-4 endpoint details, with one major exception.
- `phi_4_endpoint`: Use the following URL, populating the value of your Microsoft Foundry name for `{YOUR_AI_FOUNDRY}`: `https://{YOUR_AI_FOUNDRY}.services.ai.azure.com/models`.

<skills>
Skills provide specialized capabilities, domain knowledge, and refined workflows for producing high-quality outputs. Each skill folder contains tested instructions for specific domains like testing strategies, API design, or performance optimization. Multiple skills can be combined when a task spans different domains.
BLOCKING REQUIREMENT: When a skill applies to the user's request, you MUST load and read the SKILL.md file IMMEDIATELY as your first action, BEFORE generating any other response or taking action on the task. Use #tool:read/readFile to load the relevant skill(s).
NEVER just mention or reference a skill in your response without actually reading it first. If a skill is relevant, load it before proceeding.
How to determine if a skill applies:
1. Review the available skills below and match their descriptions against the user's request
2. If any skill's domain overlaps with the task, load that skill immediately
3. When multiple skills apply (e.g., a flowchart in documentation), load all relevant skills
Examples:
- "Help me write unit tests for this module" -> Load the testing skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Optimize this slow function" -> Load the performance-profiling skill via #tool:read/readFile FIRST, then proceed
- "Add a discount code field to checkout" -> Load both the checkout-flow and form-validation skills FIRST

The real error is clear: **`DefaultAzureCredential` failed to retrieve a token** inside the MCP server subprocess. The MCP inventory server runs as a child process spawned via `stdio`, and it needs to authenticate with `DefaultAzureCredential` too (for the `AzureOpenAI` call in `discountLogic.py`).
